sentences.text: Yes--
This thing with Nisko didn't succeeded.
The camp was dissolved.
At that time, the border between Russia-- the occupied Poland-- and German-- there was a pact between the Russia and German.
And many of the Nisko Jews had to fly to the Russian--
And in that way they were saved.
Some of them came back to Mahrisch Ostrau, and some disappeared.
Some-- I mean, were left in Poland and just were killed later on.
Yeah.
This German lady had a son who came just back from-- he was a soldier and celebrated the occupation of Paris.
And it was Friday evening, Erev Shabbat.
And he was half drunk.
And we were sitting in the--
He was a soldier in the army.
He was a soldier-- very-- but--
But he was not a high officer.
No, no, no, he was a very simple soldier.
And he came, and he saw us sitting there.
And in his drunkenness, he looked at me-- I was this little boy sitting somewhere high on a chair high.
And he said, oh, I am sorry for you because all your other-- and he looked on the other people-- you will disappear.
You will perish.
But for you, I have some compassion.
I am sorry for you.
And somehow he spoke as if-- later on-- he surely saw or witnessed what's going on in the east.
Because otherwise he could say, all of you will perish.
And then he said, I am a good German.
If I would say just one word, they would throw you out, and I can take you flat.
But I am not like this.
That was in-- at the time when Paris was--
It was such an impression that I couldn't forget it.
At that time, there was also the Heydrich affair, more or less.
No, slowly--
No, this factory was-- eventually, we had to give it away.
And things were more and more difficult.
I remember that some of the materials for the working were left, and my mother, at night, took me as a cover, because it was dangerous to go with material.
And she took me to some village, and she sold all what we could still sell.
And as a little boy, I was a cover for her.
That it wasn't so dangerous that somebody would stop her on the street.
But things were more and more dangerous.
They sometimes took Jews on the street and just took them and threw them over the border, which was not far away.
And I remember if a Jewish woman complained, what shall we do, to Gestapo-- at that time still people could somehow speak with them.
So they said, what do you want?
They asked, what shall we do?
We have no husbands.
We-- they said, so dig.
Make yourself a grave.
Oh, the Germans.
This was the advice.
Yeah.
And that was, for that time, horrible.
And also around this time we heard the first rumors about Auschwitz.
Some of these people who were taken by force or sent to Auschwitz.
After a couple of months, they got a kind of telegram.
If you would like to have the ashes of your late husband, who died on a heart failure, give so-and-so many marks, and we will give you his ashes.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
It was-- it happened to be the neighbor-- [GERMAN].
I remember his name, because the brother was sent to Auschwitz.
And he was older, and the-- his-- the other [GERMAN], was a colleague in the same class.
I must mention also, we were not allowed after the Heydrich affair to go anymore to school.
All schools were forbidden.
We had a kind of education.
But later on, it was forbidden.
And the whole education was illegal.
But we had so-called circles.
And, in small numbers, we got still some education.
The Jewish community.
And the teachers and the madrichim, the youth leaders, occupied us in this way.
I think it was about '41-- '40, or '41, or maybe '42.
And all what I remember-- that it was forbidden to go on the street with more than three people.
If there were more than three people, you could be executed.
People were hanged.
So actually the Heydrich affair had effect on life all over Czechoslovakia.
Tremendously.
Also on the Czech--
And especially on the Jews.
And daily we saw lists of people who were hanged, executed, for treason and so--
Although it was not the Jews who killed Heydrich.
Yes.
But mainly these single executions-- the names who were hanged and so on-- were mainly Czech Jews-- sorry, Czech-- Czech Christians.
And the Jews--
Were just deported.
And then we heard that people are going to ghettos or sent to the east.
And we didn't know what it meant.
And I remember, once we got a letters from the Jewish community from Poland.
Where they asked us that we should send them parcel.
That must have been in the beginning of the war-- maybe in '40, '41, or something like that.
And we really had it a little better.
We always thought that-- what I heard as a child-- well, things like that, these horrible stories we hear, could only happen in the east.
But here, in Czechoslovakia, people are more on a higher level, more cultured-- that it will never happen here.
But we sent parcels to addresses.
And I remember very well, we got even an answer--
With food and maybe a little cloth.
But mainly food.
To Poland.
To Poland.
To Jews.
To Jews.
The addresses we got from the kehila-- from the community.
And I remember the content of the letter.
She said, I am a wife of a rabbi, and now I'm living in a room with about 30 other women.
My whole possession is hanging on the nail which I have over this little something which is called a 'bed.' And your food parcel is like a straw in the ocean.
It's a kind of a last help.
And it was very touching.
And--
It was a real letter.
No, no, no, it was a real letter.
And we couldn't imagine that things could--
No, no, it was--
Surely from a little-- maybe it wasn't even a ghetto.
It was just a camp or a place where they put them in the beginning, where they still had--
Yeah.
In the beginning, it was possible.
And then I remember, there came many refugees from Slovakia.
And they told us, you know, in all other places, Jews are living on parcels.
And they are afraid they will be evacuated.
They will be put in some camps.
And try to escape, if only possible.
And I remember, at that time people still could emigrate if there was a kind of certificate or Youth Aliyah.
As I told you, my sister could go.
And the headquarter in Prague and in Vienna was run by Eichmann, for Jewish immigration.
Auswanderungsamt or something like that they call it.
And many Jews worked with Eichmann.
I helped them.
They had to work.
And I even remember my cousin-- who is now in America, and one  in Israel-- they worked with Eichmann in this office.
And when they phoned us from Prague, and they phoned us here.
Is the Gestapo Center for Jewish Emigration.
But he tried to help us also to go out.
But it was only in '39, '40.
He himself-- Eichmann let him go out.
At that time, the policy of the German was to throw out--
The Jews.
As many as possible.
Forcefully, as many as possible.
But later on, it was impossible.
I was too small--
Yeah.
My family even thought maybe to immigrate to Santo Domingo.
Because at that time, in Santo Domingo you could get some visa.
And my father bought a tropical hat I remember, and--
And a white suit because we thought--
It's good for that climate.
But unfortunately, it didn't came to it.
Yeah.
It was too late.
For one reason or another, there were more and more restrictions.
We couldn't--
I don't know that.
I couldn't say.
But my uncle got a certificate, as he was one of the Mizrachi leaders--
They're wonderful, the Mizrachi leaders.
And he could go with his family to Israel and emigrated.
In '39, it was still possible.
No, he died.
But his children-- one of his children are alive.
We heard these rumors, and then we could still read newspaper.
And we read in the newspaper, in the German newspaper, that Hitler will give to the Jews a town or something in a ghetto.
And we heard rumors that Jews are sent to this ghetto.
I had an uncle in Brunn, and he went to this ghetto.
And I also remember that some of our teachers who came from Brunn had to go to Brunn, so that they could go with the transport to Theresienstadt.
Mahrisch Ostrau came somehow later to be sent off to Theresienstadt.
And one of the reasons was that in Mahrisch Ostrau was-- the head of the Jewish community was Salo Kramer.
And the Kramer action, aktion, was to confiscate the Jewish flats which were left after the deportation, and to put it in order, and to give it over to the German.
That was one of his jobs.
And therefore, the German kept him even later.
Even when all Jews were evacuated to Theresienstadt, he and a few others were left so he could-- to care for this-- for this thing.
To take care of-- to take over all these flats.

But we heard from the Jewish community that since the Jews in Mahrisch Ostrau were the first to suffer-- their husbands were sent to Nisko in '39-- the Gestapo promised them they will be the last to be sent to Theresienstadt.
And they didn't care.
They knew what will happen anyhow in the end.
So the fact is, we were sent to Theresienstadt only in autumn '42.
There were four transport to Theresienstadt.
And it went according-- numbers-- Just a second.
Before that, was the idea to send all the Jews of Czechoslovakia to Theresienstadt together in one ghetto?
We didn't know much.
We knew only that there are transported.
Now, we know there was some transport that went straight to Riga.
A couple of thousands of Jews--
To Riga and-- or to other places and disappeared.
But later on-- and if you want, I can speak about it now-- the policy of the German together with Jakob Edelstein, who was the eldest of the Jews in Theresienstadt.
But he was also the representative of the Jewish community of Czechoslovakia-- of the [?
Judenamt-- ?]
He came originally from, I think, a little village in Slovakia.
But he worked in Prague, and he was a great Zionist.
And later on, he was the leader of the Jewish community of this Palastina-Amt.
And he came with the first group of Jews who built up Theresienstadt in '41 with the AK transport, Arbeitskommando 1 and 2.

And his idea was that if we can persuade the German that here there will be a Jewish kind of ghetto which will help the German, and will help in a way us, and we will have better possibility to survive.
And the German had their ideas.
They also needed, as we know today, a kind of cover for the pressure of the International Red Cross, who asked, where are the Jews?
What about these rumors?
So they created a town, Theresienstadt, which was good at that time for both policy.
The German thought about the possibility about showing this kind of ghetto-- the show-off ghetto.
Good conditions.

And Edelstein-- his idea was to use it, or to persuade the Germans to-- that they should give something-- a place where the Jews can be creating for the German and can have all kinds of little factories, and help the Germans.
So this idea was good for both.
And therefore, the Theresienstadt ghetto was created.
Yeah, I'm a little child.
Everything was very well organized from the Jewish community.
They prepared us.
We had to fill all kinds of formulas, exactly to write down every item, and many times.
And everything should be that even no food which could perish should be left, and everything should be in the best condition.
And how to deliver the key.
Until the last detail-- how many kilos we can take with us and-- that was-- in the last detail thought of.
I remember, as a child, I was preparing for this in my way.
I was sent to a druggery to buy vitamins.
Till today, I remember it was Vitamin B, D, C-- B and C vitamin pills.
And the batteries, which were very useful for a ghetto.
And we got anti-typhoid injections.
Vaccinations.
And we got all kinds of courses.
How to behave and how to-- like Scouts.
Months.
Yeah.
Because we were--
We were the last ones.
And the Jewish community tried to teach us all kinds of jobs who may be useful for surviving.
And slowly, this kind of--
Well, we could only take-- as far as I remember-- about 50 kilos.
For a person.
And we took on us the best clothes-- sometimes two shirts, et cetera.
And I remember very well the last day before we went to the so-called [GERMAN] or [GERMAN] from where they took us.
The last time I took a bath, because two and a half years I didn't have a bath.
Or I didn't--
No, it was the normal way before.
And I remember I put very carefully away my stamp collection.
I put it on the roof somewhere.
You hid it.
And I remember we had some appointment with my best friends, who went one transport before me, which means only a couple of days before me.
He said, surely the war will not last more than a couple of weeks or months.
And the rumors that the Germans don't have trains, and the war will be over.
And we said, you will meet me on this and this corner in three months.
By the way, years afterwards I dreamt-- and I was waiting in the railway station for this meeting.
No, no, he didn't survive.
Yeah.
Yes.
It was a very dangerous and forbidden.
But at night, we took some valises with clothes.
At that time, a suit and so on was very valuable things.
Even a golden watch, by the way, which-- everything in my case was returned.
I remember even--
I remember that my neighbor, who was a very religious man, ba'al teshuvah, he hid the Sefer Torah.
He hid the real Sefer Torah.
And he hid it somewhere, and I remembered it.
And his son survived.
And I told him where the Sefer Torah is, and he found it there, and could take it-- in the same house.
Yeah.
It was a milkman, a very simple man, who lived outside Ostrawa.
And after the war, I remembered exactly his name and the address.
And he gave it to me without any--
But I knew, of course, that to many others it didn't happen.
Very well.
I remembered it.
It was the autumn, in the eighth month, I think.
About September, I think.
And we came to a place.
And the atmosphere was horrible.
Because the very first time in my life I could somehow feel the helplessness and old people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And suddenly, you are in a place with hundreds of other peoples.
And crying and shouting.
And then somebody said-- they said, this man committed suicide.
That was my former head of our school, Mr.
Robin. ?] I remember.
And then I remember-- shortly before, I visited the headmistress of our school.
She was Mrs.
Sifer. ?] She was a very old-- in my eyes at that time-- an old lady.
And she was living in a-- at that time, before the deportation from Mahrisch Ostrau-- in an old parents' home or something.
And was so shocked for me.
It's a symbol of the best is suddenly in an old parents' home.
And later on, I saw her in Theresienstadt, which was even a terrible shock for me.
Just before the deportation, when once they took 10,000 of elderly people and sent them away.
And she was sitting alone, with her number, and waiting to be taken to Auschwitz.
And that was unforgettable.
No.
We didn't.
We-- at that time, we knew only to the east.
We didn't know about Auschwitz.
They were deported to the east.
But--
Well, I will try to tell you about it.
Just before Theresienstadt, I told you they took all kinds of things from us-- the jewelry.
And we had to help the German soldiers-- Winterhilfswerk-- WHW-- to give all warm clothes.
And even the walking sticks.
I mean, every family at the time had walking sticks and we had it from the mountains with all these kinds of signs.
We had to give it away.
And this walking stick-- somehow, I had very many memory, because I met them again in many other places.
In Auschwitz and even in the crematorium.
But now we gave everything away.
And the transport came, and we came to this place.
And we had to give away our personal identification card or whatever we had.
And it was stamped, liquidiert, liquidated.
Liquidiert.
And I don't think they took it away.
But anyhow, this stamp I remember-- liquidiert.
And after a day or so, we were put in a train.
But it was still a normal train, but full of people, and crying, and with our possessions.
And I remember even SS man came, and somebody got a kind of flick, because he didn't stood up in the first minute.
But it was a normal train.
It was still a normal train.
And we had still some food on the way.
And when we arrived to Theresienstadt, we arrived to a little village before Theresienstadt-- Bohusovice.
And then our luggage was taken away.
And of course, all the luggage was signed with the transport number.
Mine was BL214.
And they said, don't worry.
You will get it.
And some little carriers-- people took it away from us.
And we had to walk quite-- I don't know-- let's say for a half and hour, maybe longer.
And we arrived in Theresienstadt.
Then we knew this building was called [GERMAN SPEECH].
That was the [GERMAN SPEECH].
That was the place where they put us in.
They took and controlled all our possession-- I mean, the ones we had.
But we really got the other parcels.
We got them back.
And only some things they took out.
It was a way of luck.
But the moral is, we got them back.
And we came to a huge barrack.
Later on, we knew that it's the central barrack where all the high officers were.
And we-- at least my family-- came to a roof of this barrack.
It was in horrible condition.
The roof.
It was overcrowded, filthy.
And people were sitting on the floor, of course.
And the language was such a strange Austrian language from the time of the First World War.
Words which I never knew or heard before.
[GERMAN SPEECH], which means rooms or--
Toilets.
All kinds of strange names and things which we were not used to.
Or suddenly we saw German Jews.
Blind people who had a yellow kind of strip with three black circles, which was a sign for a blind people.
It wasn't in Czechoslovakia.
And for a child of just bar mitzvah, everything was new.
And also that people around-- overcrowded, terribly overcrowded, and shouting, and screaming.
And everyone wanted to find his place.
All of this was new.
By the way-- sorry-- I arrived just after my bar mitzvah.
And maybe I may go for a minute back.
Bar mitzvah was something very unusual-- oh, it's red.
